serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Khumb Matar is approximately 200 g, served as a side curry alongside rice or Indian bread.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Mushroom curry and green peas composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry for Khumb Matar exists. Values estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for cooked mushrooms (Agaricus bisporus), cooked green peas, and a standard North Indian curry base (onion-tomato-oil-spice gravy) in approximate proportions of 40% mushroom, 25% green peas, 35% curry base. Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked mushrooms (FDC ID 169251) and cooked green peas (FDC ID 170420).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — vegetable curry and legume-vegetable dish analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Khumb Matar. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous low-GI vegetable curry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Mushrooms have a very low GI (approximately 10–15); green peas have a GI of approximately 48–54 (University of Sydney GI Database). The combined dish with a curry base of onion, tomato, and spices yields an estimated blended GI of approximately 35, classified as low (≤55). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (35 × 6.8 × 2) / 100 = 4.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: Khumb Matar contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (620 mg per standard 200 g serving), which represents a meaningful contribution to the daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre from mushrooms and green peas
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 35 and 2.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g, Khumb Matar produces a low glycemic load of approximately 4.8 per serving, making it a suitable curry option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed with low-GI staples such as basmati rice or whole-wheat roti.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and cardioprotective phytonutrients from mushrooms and peas
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Khumb Matar is low in saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g) and contains zero cholesterol, with mushrooms providing beta-glucans that may support LDL reduction; it is a heart-friendly curry choice provided oil quantity is controlled during preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Khumb Matar (also spelled Khumb Mater or Mushroom and Pea Curry) is a classic North Indian vegetarian curry made from button mushrooms (khumb) and green peas (matar) cooked in a spiced onion-tomato gravy. The dish is prepared by sautéing onions, ginger, and garlic in oil, adding tomatoes and ground spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, and garam masala, then simmering mushrooms and green peas until tender. The result is a mildly spiced, earthy, and slightly tangy curry with a soft texture from the mushrooms and a gentle sweetness from the peas. Khumb Matar is a staple in North Indian home cooking and restaurant menus across India and the Indian diaspora in Singapore. Nutritionally, it is low in saturated fat, contains no cholesterol, and provides dietary fibre, B vitamins (particularly riboflavin and niacin from mushrooms), and plant-based iron. Its low glycemic index (estimated GI 35) makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The moderate sodium content from added salt warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and halal, making it broadly accessible across dietary requirements. Commonly served as a side dish with basmati rice, naan, roti, or paratha.
common_user_queries: Is Khumb Matar good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or USDA entry exists specifically for Khumb Matar as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for cooked mushrooms, cooked green peas, and a standard North Indian curry base. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using comparable low-GI vegetable curry dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data from ICMR-NIN and USDA are well-established and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN component data (cooked mushrooms, cooked green peas, curry base) and cross-checked with USDA FoodData Central. No single approved-source entry for the prepared dish exists. 2. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Khumb Matar was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish in ICMR-NIN or USDA component sources. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for these vegetable components. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish prepared with vegetable oil; no industrial trans fats are present. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based dish containing no animal-derived ingredients. 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients; B12 is naturally absent. 8. The food_name canonical form is 'Khumb Matar'; the input spelling 'Khumb Mater' is preserved as an alias. 9. Multilingual names provided for Indian cuisine served in Singapore context: Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi. 10. Serving size of 200 g is a standard restaurant portion estimate; actual restaurant portions may vary.
